chunk_summary: Holder pasteurization ensures the safety of donor milk by heating to 62.5°C for 30 minutes, followed by microbiological testing to meet release criteria. Contaminated or failed batches are discarded to maintain quality and safety.
learning_objectives: Understand the process and purpose of holder pasteurization in donor milk banking.
Identify microbiological release criteria for pasteurized donor milk.
Recognize the importance of aseptic techniques and batch traceability in milk banking.
Learn the protocols for handling contaminated or failed milk batches.
key_facts: Holder pasteurization involves heating milk to 62.5°C for 30 minutes, then cooling to 4°C within 10 minutes.
Pooled raw milk with high Staphylococcus aureus counts or gross contamination is discarded, not pasteurized.
Post-pasteurization, milk is released only if cultures show no significant bacterial growth (<10 CFU/mL).
Pasteurized donor milk is stored frozen at -20°C with an expiry of up to 6 months.
Failed batches are discarded rather than repasteurized to avoid cumulative heat damage.
reference_quote: Donor human milk for the high-risk infant: preparation, safety, and usage options in the United States — https://publications.aap.org/pediatrics/artic...
document_key_topics: Microbiological release criteria for donor milk
Handling and storage of pasteurized donor milk
Protocols for contaminated or failed milk batches
